episode_title: Vilas Dhar: Why the Future of Journalism Is Still Human

theme_codings.theme_name: The Automation Paradox: What AI Replaces, What Remains Irreplaceable

best_quote.text: an AI system cannot understand the vulnerability and pain of somebody who has just lost their house because of a bad discriminatory decision. Cannot understand what it feels like to be in the room as your son or daughter gets sentenced by an AI system for something they didn't do. Can't understand empathy or care, responsibility, or love. These are things that are uniquely human in this world.
best_quote.speaker: Vilas Dhar

theme_codings.summary: A central theme of the episode. Dhar argues passionately that AI can find patterns, source facts, and assist storytelling, but cannot replicate empathy, care, or human connection. He frames the displacement of functional tasks as potentially positive, forcing a societal reckoning about what we truly value in ourselves. He also identifies low-hanging fruit like streamlining editorial processes and automating less interesting tasks to propel broader AI literacy across the organization.

theme_codings.theme_name: Trust on the Line: Credibility, Transparency, and the Audience Contract

best_quote.text: How does a editor, a journalist, a newsroom ensure that the people that they are serving know what's AI and what isn't? And there are a number of mechanisms that I think newsrooms, some of them amazing friends of yours and mine are doing out there that range from disclosing all use of AI to maybe partnering every story, every piece of output with an explanation of how AI was used in that particular piece of reporting.
best_quote.speaker: Vilas Dhar

theme_codings.summary: Transparency is one of the three pillars of Dhar's ethical AI framework. He advocates for disclosure mechanisms ranging from full AI use disclosure to per-story explanations. He frames public trust in media as a crisis that makes audiences vulnerable to AI-enabled misinformation, and proposes verification coalitions and regional trust-building as responses. He also raises the authenticity question of distinguishing editorial voice from AI-generated content.

theme_codings.theme_name: The Ethics Infrastructure: From Principles on Paper to Practice in the Newsroom

best_quote.text: how do we set bright line rules about AI? There are things that we will definitely never use an AI tool to do. For example, the creation of a synthetic quote, right? Using an unattributed source, using LLMs to generate things that might make a story feel stronger, but violate a foundational precept of journalistic ethics.
best_quote.speaker: Vilas Dhar

theme_codings.summary: The episode's dominant theme. Dhar presents a three-part ethical framework: responsible data (provenance, representation, bias in datasets), clear boundaries (bright-line rules like prohibiting synthetic quotes), and transparency. He advocates for AI risk registers, board-level oversight, structural accountability, and the LISA framework (Listen, Involve, Share, Assess) for stakeholder-centered AI deployment. He emphasizes that ethics must be an operational input, not a secondary evaluation.

theme_codings.theme_name: Building the Machine: Technical Architecture and Engineering Decisions

best_quote.text: we don't need bigger models. We need smaller, more targeted, more intelligent models that actually give us exactly what we need and leave aside everything else. Those models are much more power effective. They're take much less water. They take much less energy to deliver on.
best_quote.speaker: Vilas Dhar

theme_codings.summary: Dhar addresses the build-vs-buy dilemma directly, arguing newsrooms must move beyond being consumers of commercial AI to building custom tools. He advocates for smaller, targeted models over massive general-purpose ones, citing environmental and efficiency benefits. He references tools like MuckRock and JournaPilot as examples of journalism-specific builds, and discusses the need for collaborative infrastructure where organizations share data and models.

theme_codings.theme_name: The Literacy Imperative: Upskilling the Newsroom for an AI Era

best_quote.text: leaders need to be asking, how do we ensure that both the user, the consumer The creator, the architect, and the person who's supposed to oversee it are all equally literate in what AI means in the organization.
best_quote.speaker: Vilas Dhar

theme_codings.summary: Dhar describes three phases of AI adoption: casual personal exploration, purpose-aligned custom use pairing broad AI capabilities with domain expertise, and building proprietary tools. He emphasizes that organizations must ensure equal AI literacy across all roles. He advocates for sandbox environments where journalists can experiment safely, and frames audience literacy as part of journalism's responsibility through behind-the-scenes reporting on AI decision-making.

theme_codings.theme_name: Organizing for AI: Leadership, Strategy, and Institutional Transformation

best_quote.text: we need to shift power back to those folks and have them be the ones who are driving implementation.
best_quote.speaker: Vilas Dhar

theme_codings.summary: Dhar calls for a fundamental organizational reckoning rather than superficial AI adoption. He argues against siloing AI implementation from journalism, advocating for cross-functional integration where reporters and journalists have editorial control over AI use. He critiques the approach of tech-magnate-led media organizations that prioritized technology over journalistic values, and calls for boards and executive teams to reassess their position on the digital adoption curve. He frames the question as 'who do we aspire to be in an AI-enabled world' rather than which vendor to pick.

theme_codings.theme_name: Power, Money, and the Platform: Big Tech and the News Industry

best_quote.text: if these big tech companies are really interested in building models that'll Maximize profits that'll serve as many as people as possible that are looking for that kind of least common denominator. Who's incentivized to build AI models exclusively for journalism?
best_quote.speaker: Vilas Dhar

theme_codings.summary: Dhar critiques the concentration of AI development among a few large providers incentivized to build massive, general-purpose tools. He argues journalism is too small a sector to attract dedicated commercial AI investment, making it essential for journalism to build its own tools. He proposes collaborative models and shared governance as counter-strategies to Big Tech dependency, and criticizes the culture of 'bigger is better' in AI development.

theme_codings.theme_name: Reimagining the News Product: Personalization, Formats, and the Experience Layer

best_quote.text: I wonder for a newsroom that's trying to deploy AI, how to take the Lisa framework and turn it into behind the scenes reporting to share with your audience here's how an article was put together. Here's all the places where AI was potentially thought about being used. Here's where we chose to do so and where we chose not to because of the ethical problems it might create.
best_quote.speaker: Vilas Dhar

theme_codings.summary: Dhar envisions journalism as not just an AI consumer but a public educator, proposing behind-the-scenes reporting on AI decision-making as a new format. He discusses AI-enabled fact-checker agents as a new audience engagement mechanism, and references tools like Gigafact for real-time verification. He also describes the JournaPilot tool that repurposes investigative journalism, suggesting new formats for content distribution.

theme_codings.theme_name: Survival Economics: Business Models, Revenue, and Market Position in the AI Era

best_quote.text: I don't think there is a way to be a media organization in this world today without also being a technology organization.
best_quote.speaker: Vilas Dhar

theme_codings.summary: Dhar argues that the false dichotomy between media organization and technology organization must be resolved. He notes that AI is accelerating the transformation timeline from 10-20 years to 18 months. He discusses the McGovern Foundation's role as a funder supporting newsroom AI adoption, and frames AI as enabling newsrooms to tell more compelling stories and reach more consumers. He raises the question of whether AI-driven efficiency gains can translate into subscriptions and profitability.

theme_codings.theme_name: Regulating the Revolution: Law, Policy, and Journalism as Accountability

best_quote.text: we're not yet at the place of saying we have best practices for each of these three domains. Instead, what we have is something that I find quite joyful and wonderful. The ability to innovate and try and pilot and test.
best_quote.speaker: Vilas Dhar

theme_codings.summary: Formal regulation is not directly discussed. However, Dhar touches on self-regulation through organizational governance frameworks, AI risk registers, and board-level oversight. He acknowledges the absence of established best practices and frames the current moment as one of industry-led experimentation rather than regulatory compliance. He calls for industry consolidation around common standards for provenance and verification.

theme_codings.theme_name: The Prototype Pipeline: From Experiment to Shipped Product

best_quote.text: organizations are going to need to iterate and evolve much faster than they've ever had to before on their technology policies. But also on their capacity to evaluate these new and incoming tools to ask questions about what they need to do to secure them. And then to intentionally create things like sandbox environments that are test beds where journalists can go out and try and play with these tools without creating the risk of new vulnerabilities.
best_quote.speaker: Vilas Dhar

theme_codings.summary: Dhar describes three phases of AI adoption that mirror a prototyping pipeline: casual experimentation, purpose-aligned custom use, and building proprietary tools. He advocates for sandbox environments as safe test beds and notes that the cost of prototyping has significantly decreased. He references specific tools (MuckRock, JournaPilot, Gigafact) as examples of journalism-specific products that emerged from this pipeline.

theme_codings.theme_name: Data Privacy, Source Protection, and the Boundaries of Responsible Use

best_quote.text: we're often barreling into trying to put new tools or platforms into place without first ensuring that we have made sure that we have real boundaries around the provenance of data. understanding who's represented in the data sets that are shown in stories.
best_quote.speaker: Vilas Dhar

theme_codings.summary: Responsible data is the first pillar of Dhar's ethical framework. He warns about data provenance, representation gaps, and unintentional exclusion of voices from datasets. He raises privacy concerns about journalists using AI tools on sensitive data about vulnerable people outside of organizational environments, creating risks of data leakage. He advocates for organizational data strategies that include vetting and securing AI tools before deployment.
